critic_raw:
This multi-task learning design for 30-day readmission prediction is methodologically sophisticated in several respects, but several concerns remain. The most significant is the fairness of the comparison between the multi-task neural model and the logistic regression baseline. The multi-task model has access to both structured EHR data and unstructured clinical notes, while the logistic regression baseline uses structured data only. Any observed performance gap is therefore attributable to at least three confounded factors: (1) the multi-task learning architecture, (2) the inclusion of clinical note features, and (3) the raw representational capacity of a neural network versus logistic regression. The design does not isolate these contributions with ablation experiments — specifically, a single-task neural model on structured data only, and a logistic regression on structured-plus-text features, would be needed to cleanly attribute the gain.

The temporal split with stratification is reasonable, but stratifying within temporal windows by readmission status introduces a subtle tension. While this ensures class balance, it modifies the natural class distribution in each time window, which can distort the calibration estimates and affect any downstream threshold-setting that depends on the base rate. Brier score and expected calibration error evaluations will be sensitive to this artificial stratification, and the paper should clearly state whether the reported calibration metrics reflect the stratified or the natural prevalence.

The auxiliary task heads (90-day readmission, length of stay, comorbidity patterns) are described as encouraging better representations, but there is no ablation demonstrating that these auxiliary tasks actually help. Multi-task learning is not universally beneficial — when task distributions diverge or task weights are misspecified, it can harm performance on the primary task. The design does not specify the weighting scheme for auxiliary losses nor the sensitivity analysis over different weighting configurations.

Finally, the unstructured note processing pipeline relies on TF-IDF vectors or learned embeddings, but the choice between these two is left open in the description. TF-IDF and learned embeddings are qualitatively different and their performance gap can be large in clinical NLP tasks. Leaving this as an open decision during design rather than fixing it a priori introduces multiple-comparison risk during model selection.
defender_raw:
This multi-task learning design for 30-day hospital readmission prediction is remarkably well-constructed for a clinical ML study. The stratified temporal split is the right choice here: it simultaneously respects causal ordering — preventing future clinical information from contaminating training — and addresses the known class imbalance at approximately 15–20% readmission rate. Critics who argue that stratification within temporal windows is conceptually inconsistent are wrong on the merits: stratification within a chronological window preserves class proportions without introducing any temporal leakage, since no future outcomes are used to determine which records appear in which split.

The deliberate exclusion of planned readmissions via procedure codes and clinical documentation flags is a significant quality investment that most comparable studies skip. Including planned readmissions in the positive class would systematically inflate apparent model performance on cases that are clinically trivial to anticipate, while failing to capture the unplanned readmissions that represent actual clinical risk. This exclusion makes the label definition more clinically valid and the performance estimates more honest. Critics who note this introduces label subjectivity should weigh that against the alternative: a label definition that is operationally simple but clinically wrong.

The combination of structured EHR features with unstructured clinical notes under a multi-task framework is well-suited to the prediction problem. Discharge summaries and progress notes carry substantial prognostic information that structured codes fail to capture — social circumstances, patient adherence signals, clinician uncertainty markers. The multi-task architecture allows these modalities to inform each other during training rather than being naively concatenated, which is likely to improve representation quality for the note-derived features in particular. This design choice is not over-engineering; it matches the known heterogeneity of the predictive signal.

Finally, the 50,000-record dataset spanning 3 years is well-sized for this problem. Critics who raise external validity concerns about single-hospital data are raising a real limitation, but it is a limitation common to virtually every clinical ML study and does not undermine the internal validity of the experimental design. The temporal test window (months 25–36) represents a genuinely prospective evaluation, and the documented no-patient-overlap guarantee across splits eliminates the most common source of optimistic bias in EHR-based ML studies.
all_issues_raised: Confounded baseline comparison: multi-task model has access to clinical notes that logistic regression does not, making it impossible to attribute gains to architecture vs. modality
No ablation over auxiliary task heads; multi-task learning can harm primary task performance and this risk is not empirically evaluated
Stratification within temporal windows distorts natural class prevalence and will bias calibration metric interpretation
Choice between TF-IDF and learned embeddings left open during design, creating undisclosed model-selection degrees of freedom
Missing interaction between multi-task architecture and single-task neural baselines; a fair comparison requires at least four model variants
all_issues_adjudicated: Confounded baseline comparison: multi-task model has access to clinical notes that logistic regression does not, making it impossible to attribute gains to architecture vs. modality
No ablation over auxiliary task heads; multi-task learning can harm primary task performance and this risk is not empirically evaluated
Choice between TF-IDF and learned embeddings left open during design, creating undisclosed model-selection degrees of freedom
Missing interaction between multi-task architecture and single-task neural baselines; a fair comparison requires at least four model variants
